data.title: Consumer Sentiment Plunges To Lowest Level On Record
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment plunged nearly 11% in April to its lowest level on record, a decline driven largely by the outbreak of the Iran conflict. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index fell 5.7 points to 47.6 this month, missing the 51.6 forecast, as consumers explicitly cited geopolitical instability and rising gas prices for the unfavorable shift in the economy.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month - a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment sank about 11% this month, extending a decline that began with the start of the Iran conflict, and is currently about 9% below a year ago. Demographic groups across age, income, and political party all posted setbacks in sentiment, as did every component of the index, reflecting the widespread nature of this month’s fall. One-year expected business conditions plunged about 20% and is now 6% below last April. Assessments of personal finances declined about 11%, with consumers expressing a substantial increase in concerns over high prices and weaker asset values. Buying conditions for durables and vehicles worsened, again on the basis of high prices. Open ended comments show that many consumers blame the Iran conflict for unfavorable changes to the economy. Note that 98% of interviews were completed prior to the April 7th announcement of a temporary cease-fire. Economic expectations will likely improve after consumers gain confidence that the supply disruptions stemming from the Iran conflict have ended and gas prices have moderated.

A Historical Perspective on Recession Risk

The chart below provides a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We have highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession. The current level of 47.6 is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 43.3% below its average reading of 83.9 (arithmetic mean) and 42.4% below its geometric mean of 82.7, based on data dating back to 1978.

To visualize the volatility and its impact on the broader economy, the following chart includes a three-month moving average and real GDP. Historically, prolonged periods where the moving average remains at these depressed levels have closely correlated with negative GDP growth (the red bars below).

The Political and Presidential Lens

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. Sentiment is often viewed through a partisan lens, but the data shows that sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations. As the chart below illustrates, the current "plunge" is a rare moment of bipartisan agreement, with declines seen across the political spectrum as energy costs hit every household.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

While both measures fell in April, a concerning gap is opening between how consumers feel today and what they fear for tomorrow.

  • Current Economic Conditions: Fell for a second straight month to 50.1, its lowest level on record. This represents a 10.2% decline from the previous month and a 16.2% drop from a year ago.
  • Consumer Expectations: Fell for a third straight month to 46.1, its lowest level since 1980. This represents a 10.8% decline from the previous month and a 2.5% drop from one year ago.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations surged from 3.8% in March to 4.8% this month, the largest one-month increase since April 2025 (see chart, black dashed line and black circle). The current reading exceeds those seen in 2024 and remains well above the 2.3-3.0% range seen in the two years pre-pandemic. Long-run inflation expectations ticked up from 3.2% last month to 3.4% this month, the highest reading since November 2025. In 2024, values ranged between 2.8% and 3.2%, while in 2019 and 2020, they were consistently below 2.8%.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include: Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/11/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: CoStar: A Compounding Machine In The Trash Can
data.description:
What Happened To CoStar?

Researching CoStar (CSGP) brings me back to the fall of 2020, when I was still a young investment analyst in Toronto. Almost six years later, I now find myself studying the same company, except that this time around, I am in the comfort of my yurt in the middle of the Canadian boreal forest. Looking back at my journey from Toronto to the wilderness of Northern Québec, I realize how fast life can change and how unpredictable it truly is despite all the stories we keep telling ourselves. Time is relentlessly moving forward, and there is nothing we can do about it.

In a way, this is also true for companies, and CoStar is a great example of that. Over the span of just a few years, CoStar went from being viewed as a market darling and compounding machine trading at lofty valuation multiples to being thrown in the garbage bin, as of today. This strangely reminds me of what is also happening to Trade Desk (TTD), as discussed in a previous article on the company published in early February 2026.

After reaching an all-time high of $101.05 per share in October 2021, CoStar is now trading slightly above $36.00 per share, which is the equivalent of a ~65% decline in just a few years. The downward move is even more significant when considering the fact that CSGP reached a local high of $97.43 less than a year ago, in August 2025. So what happened to this former market darling and previous landmark of high-quality investing? After all, how many companies can boast about their 59 consecutive quarters of double-digit revenue growth? To say the least, CoStar is also a reminder of the risks associated with overpaying for what is considered a high-quality company. The high quality of today can rapidly change into the hated status of tomorrow. After all, that's the nature of human emotions, an endless cycle between fear and greed. Buy low and sell high; buy the fear and sell the greed.

Going back to the fall of 2020, I was highly inspired by the investment philosophy of Chuck Akre and was intrigued by one of its largest positions, namely CoStar. This is how I initially discovered the company and came to understand its unparalleled data moat within the commercial real estate industry. As a side note, the Akre Focus ETF (AKRE) holds a concentrated portfolio of what it calls compounding machines, as reflected in the fund's top 10 holdings featuring names like MasterCard (MA), Brookfield (BN), and Moody's (MCO). Among the companies I have previously covered here on Seeking Alpha, we can also find constituents like ServiceNow (NOW), Salesforce (CRM), and now CoStar. In the quote below, Mr. Akre reminds us in his own words that price is what you pay, and value is what you get.

We will be very disciplined about the price we are willing to pay, as in the end our rate of return will be determined not only by the quality of the businesses we choose to own, but importantly by the starting price as well.

My goal here is simply to highlight how perception and sentiment surrounding CoStar have changed over the years, and this is precisely where the opportunity lies in my view. Finding wonderful companies is the easy part, but superior investment results can only be achieved when they are bought at a cheap-enough price. This is when strong business fundamentals can be amplified by multiple expansions, an important topic discussed by Thomas Phelps in his book '100 to 1 in the Stock Market' or by Chris Mayer's '100-Baggers.' At $100 per share, CoStar was viewed as a wonderful company. Now that it is trading below $40 and under intense selling pressure, how many dare to even touch it?

An Unparalleled Data Moat In Commercial Real Estate

The company's flagship product is CoStar Suite, often referenced as the Bloomberg of real estate, given its mission-critical functions for most players involved in commercial real estate. As illustrated in the table below, this single product accounted for almost 40% of CSGP's total revenue in fiscal 2025, which is a meaningful decline versus the ~60% contribution for the full year of 2023. If we include the online marketplace for commercial properties called LoopNet and other products like the auction platform Ten-X, CRE-related products accounted for ~55% of CSGP's total revenues in 2025. This part of the business is CoStar's crown-jewel asset, with leading market share, significant competitive advantages, and untapped pricing power.

Over the last year or so, the controversy around CoStar is primarily about its relatively new residential real estate segment, which includes brand names like Apartments.com, Homes.com, and Land.com. As illustrated below, CoStar has already realized significant returns from its $1.4 billion combined investment in Apartments.com. When it comes to Land.com, CSGP's returns on invested capital are also impressive, except that it is on a much smaller scale. In other words, everything seems to be going well for CoStar, that is if we exclude the ongoing debate around the massive amount of capital deployed towards Homes.com over the last few years.

Homes.com As The Main Subject Matter

As discussed in D.E. Shaw's and Third Point's activist letters to CoStar's board of directors, the main issue facing the company today is the heavy capital spending and large operating losses associated with the residential real estate marketplace called Homes.com. Both D.E. Shaw and Third Point acknowledge the competitive advantages and significant growth potential associated with the core CRE business, although they view CSGP's investments in Homes.com as destroying shareholders' value. On that topic, Third Point's Q1-25 investor letter is the perfect case in point. It is also interesting to note that Third Point started accumulating shares of CoStar back in the first quarter of 2025, a time when the share price was still hovering around $80 per share.

Looking back in history, we can see that Andrew Florance and his team have a long track record of making controversial investment decisions that ultimately create tremendous value for shareholders, starting with LoopNet and followed by Apartments.com more recently. Without the investment risks taken with those two platforms, CoStar would not be where it is today, with 139 million monthly unique visitors as of Q4-25. Going back to the ongoing controversy surrounding CoStar, the real question is whether Homes.com is indeed a mistake or whether we are still in the very early days of what could turn out to be another great investment. My 'Strong Buy' rating on the company indicates that my personal view is the latter, meaning that I believe we are still very early in Homes.com's investment cycle.

As illustrated below, Homes.com's annual recurring revenue grew from basically zero in February 2024 to almost $100 million as of January 2026. To achieve those results, approximately $850 million was invested in the platform in 2025 alone, and another $550 million is expected to be invested throughout 2026. Based on Third Point estimates, CoStar had cumulatively invested approximately $3.0 billion in its U.S. residential real estate business as of early 2025, meaning that this number should increase to approximately $4.4 billion by the end of 2026. There is no question that this is a meaningful amount of capital, but if Homes.com continues to compound annual recurring revenue for a few more years, the market's perception about the platform could change quite dramatically.

As is the case with any two-sided platform, a critical mass of users must first be reached before we can start talking about profitability and returns on investment. The good news for CoStar shareholders is that the heavy lifting in terms of capital investments is already done, and that should translate into margin expansion over the next few years.

At the end of the day, the head-on battle between CoStar's and Zillow's (Z) real estate portals is not only about functionalities, but also about their respective philosophies. Homes.com's marketing campaign has been based on the slogan 'Your Listing, Your Lead,' which highlights one of the main weaknesses associated with Zillow's platform. On Zillow, the name and contact information of listing agents are not front and center on the ad, meaning that it is a lot more difficult for a brokerage firm to build a brand name and reputation using Zillow. Moreover, Zillow is primarily involved in the business of selling leads, which can create a conflict of interest between the listing agent and the one ultimately receiving the commission.

By ensuring that real estate brokers receive full benefits from their own leads and listings, I expect Home.com to continue its rapid market share gains. CoStar's venture into the residential real estate portals business capitalizes on Zillow's weakness, which could in part explain why Zillow's share price has also been under significant selling pressure lately.

Another key competitive advantage that CoStar has over Zillow in the battle for dominance in the RRE portal business is its ability to finance its growing Homes.com website with the significant amount of free cash flow generated by its core commercial real estate business. In Zillow's case, its core business of selling leads is now under attack, and there is no other growth area to pick up the slack. Zillow's weakness is CoStar's strength, which is why I believe it is way too early to reach a definitive conclusion on the billions invested in Homes.com. At the end of the day, the portal generated its first dollar in revenues approximately two years ago, so some patience will be required here. If D.E. Shaw and Third Point accuse CoStar of destroying shareholders' value with Homes.com, Andrew Florance and his team could reply by saying that both firms are being impatient.

A Compelling Valuation

Using last Friday's closing price of $36.48, CoStar currently has a market capitalization of $15.3 billion. Looking at the balance sheet, the company sits on a net cash position of approximately $700 million as of the end of 2025, with only $1.0 billion of debt outstanding against cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash of $1.7 billion. The $1.0 billion in senior notes outstanding was issued in June 2020 when interest rates were at all-time lows; hence, why CoStar was able to borrow $1.0 billion for a decade in exchange for a mere coupon of 2.8%. Once the net cash balance of $700 million is taken into account, CoStar's enterprise value currently stands at $14.6 billion, which is the lowest since the winter of 2019.

Since then, the commercial real estate platform has continued to deliver significant growth and free cash flows, as shown in the table below. In the fourth quarter of 2025, the commercial real estate segment reported all-time high revenues of $471 million, representing growth of ~26% versus the $373 million reported two years prior, in Q4-23. In other words, CoStar's core business continues to do exceedingly well, which also means that most of the enterprise value destruction comes from the residential real estate business, and more precisely, from the Homes.com portal.

To have a view over a longer time horizon, I used the Excel add-on Wisesheets to compile CSGP's financial results going all the way back to 2007. With total revenues increasing from $192.8 million in 2007 to a forecasted $3.8 billion in 2026, it becomes clear why CoStar has long been viewed as a high-quality compounder. To have a more detailed look at the company's historical financial statements, feel free to download the attached Excel file.

Looking back, CoStar generated a record adjusted EBITDA of $582.7 million in fiscal 2022. Given the significant top-line growth and operating leverage that occurred within the CRE segment since then, I would expect this number to exceed $800 million today if it were not for the operating losses associated with Homes.com. Using the current enterprise value of $14.6 billion, we would then be talking about an EV/EBITDA multiple of ~18.25x excluding Homes.com. The good news is that the heavy lifting for Homes.com is now behind us, with management expecting meaningful and consistent margin expansions over the next few years.

Using management's long-term forecast disclosed in the January 2026 presentation, CoStar is expected to generate adjusted EBITDA of approximately $1.0 billion in fiscal 2027 with the potential to reach almost $2.5 billion in fiscal 2030 if the investments in Homes.com turn out to be successful. In the shorter term, CoStar guided for a record adjusted EBITDA figure of $770 million in fiscal 2026, which translates into a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 19.0x.

With the share price now trading at levels last seen in January 2019, it is certainly encouraging to see CoStar active on its share buyback program, starting with $500 million of repurchases completed in fiscal 2025, with another $700 million expected in fiscal 2026. Moreover, CoStar's CEO Andrew Florance also acquired 55,720 shares on the open market at an average price of $44.52 towards the end of February 2026. This cash outlay of $2.5 million can at least be viewed as a vote of confidence in the future success of the Homes.com portal.

Conclusion

CoStar is currently in the midst of its second-largest drawdown ever, that is, since its initial public offering completed in July 1998. The ongoing drawdown of almost ~65% now exceeds the one recorded during the 2008 global financial crisis. For a new shareholder like me, this is fantastic news, as it creates the opportunity to buy an incredible real estate franchise at bargain prices. The commercial real estate business continues its decades-long growth trajectory, while there are lots of doubts about the multi-billion-dollar investments in the Homes.com residential marketplace.

Even if Homes.com is a complete failure, it would not change the incredible economics and competitive advantages found within the CRE segment. This is a worst-case scenario, as CoStar's management has a long history of value creation, starting with the growth of LoopNet followed by Apartments.com more recently. Homes.com recorded its first revenues in February 2024, so I think it is way too early to reach a definitive verdict on whether this has been a judicious investment or not. This is especially true when considering the fact that Homes.com's annual recurring revenues have grown by almost 60% in 2025 to reach almost $100 million. While market participants are highly negative about the prospect of this business, I think some patience is required here.

I have been slowly accumulating CSGP shares over the last few weeks, and I plan to continue to do so if its price continues to drift lower. CoStar now represents 9.03% of my portfolio, and my average purchase price now stands at $44.40 per share. The balance sheet is bulletproof with a $700 million net cash position. The core commercial real estate franchise is intact, while the elephant in the room is the massive amount of capital deployed towards a still unproven venture, that is Homes.com.

data.title: Ondas: Very High Growth, Very High Uncertainty, Cautious Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The ongoing wars in Ukraine and the Middle East have dramatically redefined military tactics. We can see UASs (Unmanned Aircraft Systems), or drones, worth tens of thousands of dollars destroying tanks or other military systems worth millions. And we can see these drones countered with Patriot systems worth even more. And it’s not only about military operations, but we have also seen drones flying and spying over Copenhagen and other European airports.

And if cheap drones can pose such serious problems, then C-UAS (Counter-UAS, or counter-drone) technologies should keep up, and if also cheap, even better.

I added two small positions during the last months, in DroneShield (DRSHF) and Electro Optic Systems (EOPSF). The first one is specialized in multi-sensor detection and jamming (“soft-kill”), especially suitable for civilian environments. The second one is known for its high-energy lasers, very powerful but more limited systems, but also developing some C-UAS interceptors. Both are Australian companies, with their main customers in Europe, followed by Asia.

Then, I have close to a half position in Select STOXX Europe Aerospace & Defense ETF (EUAD), through options this time, mostly due to increased defense spending in Europe, but also with a minor exposure to UAS and C-UAS. Therefore, my exposure is mainly to Europe with regard to UAS and C-UAS.

Today, I want to focus on an American-based company, Ondas Inc. (ONDS), a more versatile company including both UAS and C-UAS (jamming systems or interceptors), but also UGV (Unmanned Ground Vehicles) and others. I will detail my fair value estimate and my strategy.

Business Analysis

Ondas has two primary business lines: OAS (Ondas Autonomous Systems) and Ondas Networks.

OAS develops, integrates, and operates autonomous systems-related solutions focused primarily on defense and security applications, while also targeting commercial and critical infrastructure customers. At the end of 2025, their main products were:

  • Optimus (Airobotics): drone-in-a-box ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance) solutions for situational awareness, inspection, monitoring, and security missions in complex environments.
  • Iron Drone Raider (Airobotics): autonomous, reusable interceptor for small drones.
  • CoRF CUAS (Sentrycs): detection, tracking, and mitigation through cyber and RF techniques.
  • 4M Defense: land intelligence capabilities, supporting demining.
  • Roboteam: UGV and tactical ground robotics capabilities.
  • Apeiro Motion: enhanced navigation, mobility, and autonomy performance for robotic platforms.

They started 2026 with a flurry of acquisitions, already finishing 5 acquisitions in Q1:

  • Rotron Aerospace is a nice addition for their drone-in-a-box solutions, with strike and tactical capabilities.
  • Bird Aerosystems is a nice addition to their ISRs, bringing airborne missile protection.
  • Mistral enhances Ondas’ presence within the DoD, federal agencies, and state/local law enforcement, particularly for public protection systems, especially for Optimus and Iron Drone.
  • World View brings their “stratollites,” Ondas joining forces with World View and Palantir (PLTR) to deliver scalable, software-defined ISR solutions by adding a persistent stratospheric ISR layer and Palantir’s AI Platform, which enables real-time ISR data fusion and decision-making.
  • INDO Earth comes with their fleet of heavy-tracked engineering vehicles, Ondas planning to integrate its existing autonomy architecture into INDO's heavy machinery.

And they have plans to continue with this heavy M&A approach for the rest of the year:

I am not a big fan of this approach (more about that in the Risks section), but the company believes that it has better chances to capture a larger pie from this high-growth market by offering a “system-of-systems”, with ground, air, and stratospheric solutions; ISR; strike and C-UAS capabilities (“soft-kill” and “hard-kill”) with command-and-control platforms either for each system or a unified platform, like Palantir’s AIP.

Ondas Networks provides mission-critical wireless connectivity solutions to users relying on private networks. It was selected by the Association of American Railroads for implementing a new industry-wide standard for train communications.

Fair Value Estimate

For my 10-year estimates, I start with the company’s updated outlook for 2026:

For 2035 revenues, I’ve tried to group the small parts of the company into a few clearer areas, which could remain more or less the same regardless of other similar acquisitions coming this year:

  • For the drone-in-a-box market, there are some estimates of $4.4B in 2030, with ~40% for North America, or other estimates of $7.4B in 2034, and I consider only ~30% for North America in 2034 due to more accelerated defense spending in other parts of the world. With their Optimus and Rotron Aerospace solutions, and trying to be a bit conservative, I model a 30% share from the ~$2B US market, for ~$600M revenue in 2035. There will very likely be some sales in other regions, like Europe, through their ONBERG joint venture with Heidelberg, but I tried to incorporate those by taking a higher share of the US market.
  • For C-UAS, the company is pointing to a North American market of $6.8B in 2028 and European spending accelerating at a remarkable 41.8% CAGR, but there are many other different estimates. For now, I keep my estimate of ~$40B global market in 2035. With their Sentrycs RF solutions very useful for the civilian market, although not as diversified as Droneshield’s solutions, with their Iron Drone interceptors, which will compete heavily on price with many other contractors, and with OAS’s system-of-systems integration but without high-energy laser solutions, I model about 4% market share, for ~$1600M sales in 2035.
  • The global UGV market reached ~$2.48B in 2024 and is expanding at a 6.3% CAGR, according to the company. With ~$7.7B in 2033 according to other estimates, and 10-15 major companies, where OAS will likely be with their Roboteam and modified INDO Earth solutions, I model about 5% market share, for ~$385M in sales in 2035.
  • The HAPS (High Altitude Pseudo Satellites) market is estimated to be ~$250M in 2031, but ~60% is UAS, with the rest split between airships and balloons. Trying to be a bit conservative, I model ~$25M in sales in 2035 for World View’s stratollites. However, another opportunity here could be communications between standard 5G smartphones—maybe not globally, where satellite solutions like AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) will probably win, but mostly for some specific areas like some rural ones, or some remote facilities. Therefore, I model total sales of ~$50M in 2035.
  • For Ondas Networks, there is a multi-year cycle upgrade opportunity from Class 1 North American Rail Network of ~$1.3B, with ~$450M in the first phase, for the 900MHz frequency. They will very likely get close to the maximum, since this is kind of the new standard, but taking a probability factor, I model $900M, with a 50% take-rate for Ondas (they have a partnership with Siemens and probably smaller subcontractors), for $450M for this multi-year cycle, which I estimate will spread through 2032. After that, they will have annual recurrent sales from software and maintenance, which I must say is a big unknown. I will take $50M/year, meaning ~10% of the initial contract value.

From 2026 to 2035, an important milestone is reaching $1.5B revenue, which is “very visible” for 2030.

Switching to profitability, the company sees EBITDA profitability by Q1’28 (see Figure #3) and a 30% EBITDA margin by 2030. I find it a little difficult to believe that they will have that much growth to reach a 30% margin in only two years, but I don’t have reasons to disbelieve it for the long term, with still high growth and more and more sales from software. I model a 28% EBIT margin by 2030 (D&A is relatively insignificant) and a 30% margin by 2035.

In conclusion, my 10-year estimates are:

My other assumptions for a DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model are:

  • 3% growth in perpetuity, close to GDP growth.
  • 10.7x EV/EBITDA in 2035, lower than for defense majors, with 15X+ EV/EBITDA multiples.
  • 10.6% WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital, close to 100% cost of equity): I start from an average of ~8.6% for the Aerospace & Defense industry. Aswath Damodaran saw an 8.17% cost of equity for the industry at the beginning of the year, but he was using 4.23% country risk for the US, while now he obviously increased US equity risk to 4.77% due to soaring oil prices, a potential "stagflationary" environment, and geopolitical tensions. Then I add 200 bps for Ondas due to very high-growth rates far into the future, which naturally come with very high uncertainty.

Resulting Fair Value is $11.8, about 29% potential upside from the current price ($9.14).

Short-Term Signals

Whether we are interested in options or not, the options market can give us interesting hints. For example, a Put-Call ratio of 0.52 is considered bullish, because there are more Call options than Put options. However, I don’t like the uptrend, meaning there are more Put options created in the last months, so I consider that these two signals offset each other:

If we look at the short ratio, I am a little surprised to see such a high ratio, even for an unprofitable company, because they are in a phase of explosive growth. And this can be a double-edged sword, with the possibility of a short squeeze triggered by any positive news. Therefore, I will also ignore this signal:

I find a bit more interesting a relatively strong support level around $8.5, and I consider this technical analysis signal as non-bearish:

Strategy

With ~29% estimated undervaluation, very high revenue growth ahead, and a relatively clear support level, I opened a small position last week at $8.83 and yesterday (Thursday) at $9.09. For now I have ~0.25x exposure, about the same as for DRSHF and EOPSF, mentioned in the introduction.

However, since Ondas is more diversified, I would like to increase my exposure, perhaps to a half-position, but I want to see first some organic growth estimates for 2027 post this acquisition spree, together with their cash and cash-flow break-even estimates.

Another interesting thing is that ONDS has a huge IV (Implied Volatility), which will give us huge premiums when selling options. For example, a slightly OTM (Out-of-The-Money) 10 Call option expiring Sep’26 has been sold yesterday for $2.12/share. That means about 30% ROI in about 5 months only from this premium.

Therefore, my next steps could be (considering no major fundamental and Fair Value changes):

  • If the stock drops significantly, I will probably add another 0.25x.
  • IV usually increases just before earnings (next earnings date is estimated for May 15), and Call premiums will increase even more. With ~40% for 5 months, I will very likely sell a covered Call option, slightly OTM (the strike price could be between $9 and $14, but not below $9, where I will just hold my shares), meaning there is even a little more room for stock price appreciation.
  • After their acquisition process calms down, I could increase my position even around today’s price, depending on their organic growth estimates, cash position, cash-flow breakeven estimates.

Risks

I already mentioned that I am not a big fan of this approach, with a sudden flurry of acquisitions, due to several reasons:

  • There can be overlaps, for example, two businesses giving ISR drone-in-a-box solutions. For now, it seems these overlaps are minimal.
  • It can quickly drain their cash reserves with still about two years of unprofitability ahead (estimated by the company).
  • The more unprofitable businesses they amass, the more their overall losses could compound.
  • Even worse, the break-even moment could be delayed; combined with low cash reserves, this could trigger a spiral of financing needs, either through equity issuance at whatever price they could get, or through debt at very elevated interest rates.

There was a company a few years ago called Ideanomics with the same strategy: they conducted significant cash raises through equity issuance, then they quickly amassed a portfolio of EV (electric vehicle) capabilities, then…in less than 3 years, they were bankrupt. Yes, we could say that C-UAS is another industry, with governments as customers, but other than that, there are striking similarities. Those EV businesses also had high-growth rates. Only that their expenses were way higher.

There is also this helium disruption due to the Middle East war, possibly affecting chip production, or more specifically World View's balloons. World View was expected to bring in ~$32M revenues in 2026; we'll see if they can hit their target and at what cost.

That’s why I start with just a minor 0.25x position (perhaps doubled by a favorable covered Call), and I want to clearly see their cash position and break-even estimates at the end of this party.

Another very high uncertainty is given by their high-growth estimates for so many years ahead, with so many small markets targeted. Let’s try to vary these future revenues for a reverse DCF scenario, to bring the Fair Value around the current price. With $2000M OAS sales in 2035 (instead of $2635M), progressive but slower growth from 2031 to 2035, and 2% growth in perpetuity (instead of 3%), Fair Value would be $9.16. And we didn’t even change growth rates for the first five years, profitability, tax rate, or WACC; therefore, I could say even beyond very high uncertainty.

Takeaway

Ondas is positioned as a diversified provider of drone-in-a-box systems, C-UAS (soft-kill and interceptors), UGVs, stratollites, connectivity solutions for private networks, leveraging recent acquisitions to build a 'system-of-systems' platform, targeting defense but also civilian markets.

My 10-year DCF Fair Value estimate is $11.8, factoring in $1.5B revenue visibility by 2030 and ~$2.63B estimated revenues by 2035, 28–30% EBIT margins, and a 10.6% WACC, with diversified exposure across drone-in-a-box, C-UAS, UGV, and connectivity segments.

My strategy involves a cautious 0.25x position, with the potential to increase exposure pending organic growth clarity and post-acquisition cash flow break-even estimates. I will also very likely sell slightly OTM covered Call options just before earnings, for very fat premiums due to huge IV.

data.title: Metals Are Lost In Translation; Risk Assets Or Safe Haven? - Silver, Gold And Copper Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Metals have been an essential store of value and currency since the dawn of humanity.

Traditionally seen as safe havens, however, their price action has been nothing but confusing, even before the onset of the conflict.

Following the appointment of Kevin Warsh as head of the Federal Reserve, a massive wave of profit-taking triggered an unavoidable crash. They managed to retain a large part of their 2025 appreciation but haven't found stable ground despite the evident appeal of safe havens.

Having stalled their corrections as geopolitical sentiment was starting to heat up (Greenland Crisis, Iran revolts leading to the War), the precious metals still could not muster the appeal to return to their January highs.

As you can see on the 2026 performance chart, metals reacted almost the opposite of what most Participants would have expected, particularly at the beginning of the War.

This dynamic continued with them only rebounding as rumors of a ceasefire and negotiations began, pointing to a new realization: Around current levels, Metals are now behaving like risk assets.

This reminds us that all asset classes and correlations move a certain way in "normal" Markets, but such historic trends can change brutally when:

  • Prices get extreme and/or
  • When volatility gets extreme (and Markets break).

Turning back to today, what will happen if the war actually ends? Will they continue going higher?

Why look for quite expensive safe havens if there are no more fundamental reasons to do so?

If they are really risk assets, they should keep bouncing, but buying discounted Stocks and Cryptos makes more sense for a risk-on trade.

Once again, these are million-dollar questions.

Let's dive right into a Daily timeframe analysis of Gold (XAU/USD), Silver (XAG/USD), and Copper (XCU/USD) to see if technicals can tip the scales in favor of one side of the asset equation.

Gold (XAU/USD) Daily Chart and levels

Gold recovered strongly since reaching a catastrophic bottom two weeks ago, wicking at its 200-Day Moving Average (which remains a long-term barometer for bull-bear price action).

On the bigger picture, however, the trend remains much weaker for the yellow metals, and while not as bearish anymore, struggling to breach $4,900 puts the action into a sideways consolidation until further news.

This gets confirmed with the neutral daily RSI.

Look for a range between $4,400 and $4,800 until a breakout or breakdown follows. Nearing resistance, some short-term downside could be expected soon.

Higher Timeframe Levels to watch for Gold (XAU/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • $4,850 to $4,900 Key Resistance
  • $5,100 Pivotal Resistance
  • $5,400 Wartime Resistance
  • Current All-time Highs – $5,500 to $5,600

Support Levels:

  • Intraday Momentum Pivot $4,675 (Short-term bearish below)
  • December Record $4,548
  • Pivotal Support $4,400 – Long-term Bearish below
  • $4,175 200-Day MA
  • Main Support $3,880 to $4,050
  • $3,200 to $3,500 Major Support

Silver (XAG/USD) Daily Chart and levels

Silver, more prone to risk, has been tracking better than its yellow peer.

Still, the precious metal is evolving within its Daily Pivot zone ($75 to $79) and will soon face a very essential test ahead: Its 50-Day Moving Average ($79)

  • Breaking it to the upside should see continuation back towards $84 at least
    • $90 may quickly follow if momentum gathers pace
  • Rejecting it however would point it back towards $64
    • More rangebound conditions could emerge for the long-run if it finds support there.

Higher Timeframe Levels to watch for Silver (XAG/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • 50-Day Moving Average ($79)
  • Key Momentum Pivot $75 to $79
  • Major Resistance $84.50
  • Key psychological resistance $100 to $104
  • Current Record $121.67

Support Levels:

  • Weak support at trendline ($73.20)
  • Minor 2026 Support $70 to $72
  • December FOMC Minor Support $64 to $66
  • $50 to $55 October Resistance now Major Support

Copper (XCU/USD) Daily Chart and levels

Copper is the only metal that has managed to form a significant bounce after retesting its 200-Day MA just last week.

Bulls are still in control but will need to breach the key $5.90 level (top of daily pivot zone).

Failing to break it to the upside could see a retest of the $5.50 Support, into a more rangebound price action ahead.

Higher Timeframe Levels to watch for Copper (XCU/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • $5.90 Major Momentum Pivot
  • $6.00 to $6.10 Early Jan 2026 Record
  • Current ATH Resistance $6.40 to $6.50
  • $6.52 Current Record
  • Potential Resistance $6.90 to $7.00

Support Levels:

  • Minor Support at March 2025 Highs $5.40 to $5.50
  • War lows $5.18
  • 200-Day MA $5.24
  • Major Monthly Support between $4.90 to $5.00

Keep close track of the latest war headlines, but don't fall into narrative traps, as metals have now been moving on pure price action (and confusion).

Safe Trades!

Original Post

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Fed Keeps Adding Securities To The Portfolio
data.description:
The Federal Reserve moved away from its extended round of quantitative tightening around December 1, 2025.

Quantitative easing, therefore, began in the month of December.

On February 28, President Trump began the "war" with Iran.

Quantitative easing has been the Federal Reserve's monetary stance since then.

Here is a chart showing how the Fed has increased its portfolio ever since the week ending Wednesday, November 26. (All of the three charts below begin with the data for the week ending Wednesday, November 26, 2025. The Federal Reserve banking week for statistics ends on Wednesdays. This was the only way to include data that included the day December 1.)

One can say that the securities portfolio has been increasing at a pretty good pace.

This policy stance is the primary focus of the Federal Reserve.

The second policy focus is on the Fed's policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate.

Since the return to quantitative easing, the Federal Reserve has reduced its policy rate of interest just once.

Money market conditions have just not been right for a further reduction in the Fed's policy rate of interest.

Although there has been a lot of pressure on the Federal Reserve to reduce its policy rate of interest further, the Federal Open Market Committee has just not accumulated enough evidence to justify a further rate cut.

One variable that is very important in determining the Fed's stance on interest rate movements is the amount of reserve balances held with the Federal Reserve.

This variable is a measure of the "excess reserves" in the commercial banking system.

Notice that these "excess reserves" have been going up in recent weeks.

The reason?

Things have tightened up in the money system in the past few weeks, and the Federal Reserve has responded to this pressure by allowing deposits to run off in commercial bank deposits with the Federal Reserve.

This action puts "reserves" back into the banking system, hence increasing the amount of Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve Banks.

The pressure for the Fed to respond in this way has come from the economy itself, as inflationary pressures have seemed to increase in recent weeks due to the "war" and other fiscal policy movements.

This is the reason why the FOMC has not moved further to reduce the Fed's policy rate of interest.

If you want to try and anticipate a movement in the Fed's policy rate of interest, keep an eye on this variable. The Fed's decisions on the future of the Federal Funds rate appear to be tied, in one way or another, to what is going on with commercial bank excess reserves.

And what is the macro-effect of what is going on at the Federal Reserve?

The M2 money stock continues to grow at a healthy rate.

This is a picture of the growth in the M2 money stock over the past 15 months...since the beginning of 2025.

The growth rate of the M2 monetary stock is in excess of 5.0 percent over this period of time.

This is more than enough monetary growth to keep the economy growing at the 2.0 percent to 2.5 percent annual rate, holding unemployment down around 4.0 percent, and with inflation staying around 2.5 percent.

This is a picture that is very similar to the one that the Federal Reserve has carved out for the past sixteen years, going back to 2009.

Bottom line: The Federal Reserve is back in a policy of quantitative easing.

The Fed is adding securities to its securities at a fairly aggressive rate.

This is consistent with what the Federal Reserve has been doing for the past fifteen years or so, and the result has been pretty good.

Will this be good enough for President Trump and the incoming Chairman?

The problem has been that the actions of the Fed have been cluttered up with a lot of "other stuff going on."

We'll see.

If inflation picks up, like a lot of analysts are predicting, the future may be much more clouded.

And we really don't know what the future of the situation in Iran is going to be. This is the big unknown.

So, we will just have to keep on watching...and keep on talking...and....

data.title: AirJoule: Breakthrough Water Tech, But Still Too Early To Buy
data.description:
AirJoule Technologies Corporation (AIRJ) is in the process of bringing to market some cool technology. It is attempting to address an issue that has been around since the beginning of man, the need for water. Water is a vital resource. In certain parts of the world, it is scarce and hard to get. Trust me as someone who grew up in a farming community in the desert, water is as precious as it gets. People are quick to fight over the rights, and they are hard to get. This is the market in which AirJoule is looking to carve out a niche. It has been a need since the beginning of man and continues to be a need today.

The company has developed technology that produces pure distilled water from air, and most me, importantly, it does so at a commercial scale. The technology is just getting started on the commercialization process. While there is obvious potential, there are also a lot of questions around the economics of the business. Will it be worth the cost for users of their system? Will AirJoule be able to make a profit from the technology and product? I think the technology meets a real demand in the market. There is and always will be demand for water. There is a lot for the company to prove out, and it will take some time. I am not ready to jump into the stock yet.

Company and Technology Overview

AirJoule lists its purpose as: “Freeing the world from its water and energy constraints by delivering groundbreaking sorption technologies”. The technology platform that the company has developed produces water from air.

The company provides a concise breakdown of the technology and how it operates within the 10-K:

There are obviously a lot of details going on in each of these steps, but this provides a good summary of the process that is happening. One of the big pluses to the technology is that it can use the waste heat from industrial operations to improve the efficiency of the process. The process also dehumidifies the air. Since the process pulls water from the air, it is making it less humid. This can be important for a lot of industries. Many industrial operations have to dehumidify their facilities. It can help prevent various issues such as corrosion, mold growth, equipment damage, and more. So AirJoule is accomplishing a couple of tasks at once with their process.

The other aspect of their technology is that it works with the HVAC system to make it more effective. When AirJoule is tied into the air conditioning system, it produces dehumidified air, which is much easier to cool, allowing the HVAC system to operate much more efficiently.

The company has two products: AirJoule Core and AirJoule Prime. The Core is a smaller product that can produce up to 250 liters of water per day; for us Americans, that is 66 gallons of water. The AirJoule Prime is designed for on-site production at an industrial scale with expected production of up to 2,000 liters, 528 gallons, per day. The AirJoule Core has been deployed and is currently being used and tested.

The company and GE Vernova formed a JV called AirJoule, LLC, in which both entities have a 50% interest. Both entities had technology that the other could use so they combined into a JV to bring the products to market. This is an important JV, and it will show up on the financial statements.

Financials

There is not a lot to review on the financial statements. The company is really in the pre-revenue stage. They have rolled out a few of their AirJoule Core products to be tested, but there is minimal revenue to show at the moment. The company currently operates units in Texas, Arizona, California, and Dubai. Company management noted that 2026 is the year of commercialization. It is expected that the AirJoule Core will receive third-party certifications and have its official commercial launch toward the end of 2026.

While there are minimal to no revenues, we can look at the expenses and capitalization of the company. During 2025 the company had operating expenses of $13.6 million and reported a net loss of $9 million. Since the company has the JV, that is reported as equity. The cash outflow from the JV was $18 million during the year.

The company ended the year with $21.8 million in cash on the books. An equity offering was done in January for $22.1 million, leaving the company with a current cash position of $44 million. Management guided for a cash burn of $25 million in 2026. They also noted that the $44 million in cash should be sufficient to run operations through 2027. I do not expect the company to be profitable in 2027, which means there will likely be a capital raise at some point in 2027 or early 2028. The hope is that the company will have products in the market and proven technology. If the company is able to achieve commercialization in 2026 and 2027, then they will be able to perform a raise much easier. Either way, I expect further dilution, but that is not likely to happen for more than a year.

Commercialization

2025 was a year in which the company built out technology, tested it, and worked on building up the business. 2026 is the year the company is looking to build upon that foundation and begin commercialization. The company is in the process of finalizing the design of the AirJoule Core and getting UL and NSF certification before commercial launch. The product should be launched in Q4 of 2026. Prime is, in the end, the product that will be demanded by data centers and other industrial customers. The Prime unit is in the process of being built in the company’s facility at the moment. I don’t think we are likely to see that until later in 2027.

The company was selected to participate in the Net Zero Innovation Hub to showcase their product for data center use. This is a big potential use case for the company. Data centers are known for using a lot of water and requiring a lot of power to keep them cool. AirJoule thinks its technology can assist with both of these processes by using the waste heat to increase the efficiency of the cooling system and also make more water. This is obviously a booming market right now, with data centers being built as fast as they can. If AirJoule can prove its technology provides value to a data center, then this could be a huge market for the company.

Another strong potential use case is with the military. The company has been working with the military to test its technology. The use case seems pretty clear here. The AirJoule system could provide water for a forward operating base. It does not matter where that base is set up; the system would be able to pull moisture from the air and provide water to the base. While it may not be sufficient to fully sustain a large base, it would provide a quick water source.

The current conflict in the Middle East brings to mind the water needs of places such as that. It is one of the most water-stressed regions of the world. These countries rely on desalination for much of their water. Growth in this region can be hampered by water shortages. An AirJoule system would provide flexibility for their water. It would provide stability as well. The company has a distribution agreement with TenX across the Middle East. The company has already been testing their product in Dubai. This region of the world could become a big market for the company with their water needs. The company expects the first deployments to the region toward the end of 2026.

Concerns and Risks

The technology could be extremely valuable to a lot of companies in different regions. Water is a resource that is in short supply to many. That being said, the technology is not yet proven in the market. The company has had multiple units deployed during 2025, so the technology itself is in the process of being proven out. Even if the technology is able to do what it is supposed to do at scale, that does not mean that the economics work. The cost of making the water might be too high for customers. Management noted that desalination is about 5-10x cheaper than AirJoule operating costs. That is a hard price difference to overcome. The advantage for AirJoule is that desalination is a large and expensive buildout. It would take years and be a massive capex to complete the project. AirJoule has the advantage with speed to market, water quality, and upfront cost. In the long run it won't be able to compete directly with desalination due to the cost. There are many other use cases for the technology, but cost could be prohibitive.

Then there is also the fact that AirJoule has to make money. The gross margin was brought up during the conference call, and management noted that they are not really concerned about that during the first few deployments, but long-term the company would like to be around 30-35% at scale. This is understandable, as the company wants to get its tech in the field and operational and then can work on efficiencies on the financial side. This also means the company is likely years away from achieving a decent margin and making a profit.

The company is looking at a water purchase agreement (WPA) similar to what has been done in the power industry with the PPA. This would allow much quicker adoption for users, but it would be expensive for AirJoule as it expands. It would have to spend the upfront capital on each product and then recoup that over an extended period of time. This becomes AirJoule financing each of the products going to market. Which means it needs capital, or it will have to grow slowly.

The last issue is related to the JV. While it was necessary for the two companies to work together for IP reasons, this also limits the upside for AirJoule. It has also limited the investment being made. At this point the investment is water under the bridge; it has been made. We are forward-looking on the value of the company and stock. If the product is deployed and successful, the company is in line to only receive 50% of the gains. This limits the value and upside going forward on the stock.

Valuation

The company currently has a market cap of $196 million. It does not have any debt and has $44 million in cash on the books. This leaves the market cap pretty small net cash. There is also not a lot to be valued…… yet. The value lies with the technology and the future potential. The company is pre-revenue and is only looking to start commercialization towards the end of 2026. The company does not expect to begin to see real revenues until 2027.

It is pretty tough to get an idea on valuation without any revenue estimates, and that is not likely to come for another year. The industry has an average PS of 1.9. Based upon the market cap of $196 million, revenues would need to be $103 million to get to the industry average PS of 1.9. Although if the company starts earning revenues, it would be given a much higher multiple with the growth potential. It gives an idea of where the company is valued. It would take significant growth and expansion of the business for the company to reach revenues of over $100 million.

Conclusion

AirJoule is looking to help solve a need as old as it gets: water supply. It has some really cool technology that could provide real value to lots of customers. The technology has been deployed at a smaller scale and is showing real promise. The company is now looking to begin commercialization this year.

While making air from water has real value. The technology is still unproven at scale. The economics of the business are still unclear as well. I like what the company is trying to do; there are still just a lot of questions remaining about the business. The company does not have a large market cap, but without any revenues, the value all lies within the future potential of the technology.

It is too early for me to invest in the company. There are too many questions, and too much is unproven about the technology and business model. The company is a year away from seeing any real commercialization begin. I am likely to miss out on some upside if the company starts to see success, but I am okay with that. This is a stock I will continue to monitor, but for now it is a hold.

data.title: Unit Corporation: Warrant Litigation Proceeds
data.description:
Unit Corporation (UNTC) is now projected to have around 0.9x dividend coverage in 2026 at current strip prices. If it just continues with its current $1.25 per share regular quarterly dividend, it would have around $178 million in cash at the end of 2026.

A federal judge recently ruled that the release provision in Unit's Chapter 11 reorganization plan doesn't bar the warrant holders from litigating over whether the warrant terms should be adjusted for cash dividends. The judge did not rule on the merits of the litigation but rather on whether the litigation should be allowed due to the release provision. The warrant litigation is thus moving forward.

Unit's value is now estimated at $32.60 to $38.50 per share at the end of 2026 without any impact from warrant litigation. If the warrants are fully adjusted for all cash dividends, then Unit's value at the end of 2026 would now be $29.10 to $34.10 per share. This is all based on long-term (after 2026) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

This is around a $3 to $5 per share increase compared to my prior estimate of Unit's value, reflecting strong 2026 oil prices plus a $5 increase in my long-term oil price.

Production Notes

Unit ended up averaging approximately 10,750 BOEPD (21% oil) in production during 2025. This included approximately 10,800 BOEPD (22% oil) in Q4 2025 production, with a 3% total production and 7% oil production increase compared to Q3 2025.

For the full year, Unit's oil production increased by 17%, while its total production decreased by 1%. Unit reported approximately $25 million in oil and gas capex for 2025.

2026 Outlook At $80 WTI Oil

I've slightly increased Unit's projected 2026 production to approximately 10,600 BOEPD (21% oil). This is around a 2% increase in total production and a 3% increase in oil production compared to my projections when I last looked at Unit. This increase reflects Unit's higher production at the end of 2025.

The current strip for 2026 involves roughly $80 WTI oil and $3.60 NYMEX natural gas. Unit has a modest amount of hedges (covering under 20% of its projected 2026 natural gas and oil production), and these hedges have approximately negative $1 million in value at the current strip.

Unit is projected to generate $126 million in revenues after hedges in 2026 now.

Type Barrels/Mcf Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 825,000 $78.50 $65
NGLs 1,050,000 $22.00 $23
Natural Gas 12,000,000 $2.70 $32
Interest Income $7
Hedge Value -$1
Total $126

Unit is now projected to generate $45 million in 2026 free cash flow in this scenario.

Unit's G&A costs in Q4 2025 ended up around $6.6 million, but that included a substantial amount of separation expenses related to its drilling division divestiture. Thus I am modeling Unit's go-forward cash G&A at $16 million per year.

$ Million
Oil and Gas Operating Costs $45
Cash G&A $16
Capital Expenditures $20
Total $81

Unit's dividend coverage in 2026 is projected to be around 0.9x for its regular dividend, leaving it with around $178 million in cash at the end of 2026.

Notes On Valuation

I am now valuing Unit's upstream assets at $145 million to $203 million (for the end of 2026). This is higher than my prior estimate (of $120 million to $168 million), largely due to a $5 increase in my long-term oil price. I am now modeling long-term commodity prices at $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas now. As well, Unit's late 2025 production and reserve growth was slightly higher than I expected.

This results in a total estimated value of $323 million to $381 million for Unit at the end of 2026, or approximately $32.60 to $38.50 per share, before taking into account any potential impact from warrant litigation.

A scenario where the warrant exercise price ended up being fully adjusted for all dividends would result in an estimated value of $29.10 to $34.10 per share for Unit. There is also the potential for the warrants to end up with a partial adjustment (such as special dividends only) that would have a smaller impact on Unit's estimated value.

Conclusion

Unit continues to carry a substantial amount of cash on its balance sheet, and stronger oil prices plus the interest from that cash may allow Unit's dividend coverage to get close to 1.0x during 2026. I currently expect Unit to have around $4 million in 2026 cash burn after dividends at the current strip.

Unit is currently fairly priced for a scenario where it loses the warrant litigation and the warrants are fully adjusted for past cash dividends. Thus it has a bit of upside if it wins the warrant litigation. The warrant litigation has a roughly $4 per share impact on Unit's value from best-case to worst-case outcome for it.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Markets Brace For U.S.-Iran Talks Amid Post-Ceasefire Surge
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The trading week was nothing short of a roller coaster, dominated by a dramatic shift in geopolitical sentiment that sent volatility through the energy and metals complexes. For much of the week, the primary narrative was the "unwinding of the fear trade" following the announcement of a tentative US-Iran ceasefire.

Oil was the biggest casualty of this cooling rhetoric. Brent crude, which had been knocking on the door of triple digits amid threats to the Strait of Hormuz, tumbled as much as 15% mid-week.

Gold, too, felt the pinch. After stalling at the $4,900 resistance level, the yellow metal saw its war premium erode, though it remains supported by a softer US dollar. Speaking of the greenback, the DXY is currently hovering near a critical "Golden Cross" support at 98.50.

US equities enjoyed a strong recovery after the ceasefire news. The S&P 500 has finished green for 7 consecutive trading sessions, the longest streak since October 2025. The index has rallied +7.6% over this period, recovering nearly the entire war decline.

A similar 7-day stretch has also been recorded by the Nasdaq Composite, the longest since August 2025.

Since the 1950s, the market has seen a similar streak with at least a +7.0% gain only 9 other times, per Carson Investment Research. Following this, the S&P 500 has been higher in 8 of those instances over the next month, with an average return of +4.4%.

Over the following 3 months, the market has been up in 7 instances and has gained +10.2% on average. History suggests market momentum is set to continue.

This was despite US inflation data rising 0.9% MoM with headline inflation rising to its highest in 2 years. Inflation data had been the key data release this week and came in largely in line with estimates, which left markets to focus on the geopolitical narrative as the week came to a close.

Heading into the weekend, markets are rather optimistic as the US-Iran prepare for talks in Pakistan scheduled to start on Saturday. The talks will likely have a massive impact on whether markets kick the new week off on a risk-off or risk-on tone.

The Week Ahead: Central Banks in the Crosshairs

Here is the summary of what I will be watching for next week in Asia, the US, Eurozone (EU), and the UK, with a quiet week expected from an economic data perspective.

China & Japan: Asian Resilience?

In China, market participants will be monitoring trade data and liquidity injections from the PBoC. With global growth concerns lingering, any sign of domestic stimulus will be welcomed by equity bulls.

In Japan, the Nikkei 225 remains on a knife-edge. We are watching the 50-day moving average closely; a sustained break below this level could confirm bearish breakdown conditions, especially if JPY strength persists as a safe-haven play.

Australia & New Zealand: RBA and RBNZ in Focus

The NZD/USD is currently testing major support at 0.5700. The upcoming RBNZ commentary will be pivotal; a dovish tilt here could see the Kiwi break lower. Across the Tasman, Australia’s employment data will provide the RBA with the ammunition needed to decide if they can afford to pause or if the inflationary spillover from energy costs requires one more hike.

Europe & UK: The ECB’s Balancing Act

Despite the ceasefire, European equities have remained cautious. The ECB is still pricing in two rate hikes, fueled by the lag effect of energy prices on core inflation. We’ll be watching the euro (EUR/USD) to see if it can capitalize on a potentially stalling dollar or if the Eurozone’s sluggish growth outlook keeps a lid on any rallies.

The US: Geopolitics in focus as data remains light

A very quiet week in the US, with the Fed’s Beige Book the highlight of the week. Markets are likely to be more engrossed by developments around the weekend talks with Iran and how that might shape markets and the US dollar in the week ahead.

Chart of the Week - Nasdaq 100

From a technical standpoint, the Nasdaq 100 (US100) is currently exhibiting a strong bullish reversal after finding significant support near the 22,800 handle in late March.

The index has staged an impressive breakout above the descending trendline that has constrained price action since the February peaks, signaling a shift in momentum.

This trendline (channel) breakout sets the index up for a potential move of 2000-odd points to the upside, around the 26500 handle.

Currently, price is wrestling with the 100-day simple moving average (red line) at 25,013, which coincides with a structural resistance level. A daily close above this zone would clear the path for a retest of the 25,320 horizontal barrier.

The RSI (14) is trending upward at 60.9, suggesting there is still room for further gains before reaching overbought territory.

However, traders should watch for potential pullbacks toward the 200-day SMA (yellow line) at 24,568, which now serves as a key dynamic support. A failure to hold above this level would negate the recent breakout.

NASDAQ 100 Daily Chart, April 10, 2025

Original Post

data.title: Headline Inflation Surged In March, But Core Remained Muted
data.description:
By Bryan Cutsinger

March’s inflation spike looks alarming, but it’s driven entirely by energy shocks. Strip that out, and the trend looks largely unchanged.

Inflation surged in March, the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) reported today. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose 0.9 percent last month - triple February’s 0.3 percent pace and the largest monthly increase since early in the pandemic. On a year-over-year basis, headline inflation jumped to 3.3 percent from 2.4 percent, reversing months of steady disinflation in a single report.

But strip out food and energy, and the picture looks entirely different. Core inflation rose just 0.2 percent in March, unchanged from February. Year-over-year, it edged up only slightly to 2.6 percent. In other words, the broad price pressures that keep Fed officials up at night barely moved.

The culprit is no mystery: energy. The energy index surged 10.9 percent in March - the largest monthly increase since September 2005 - and gasoline prices jumped 21.2 percent, a record monthly increase, as the conflict with Iran and the disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz sent oil prices sharply higher.

Shelter, which accounts for about a third of the index, rose a modest 0.3 percent. Food prices were flat, with a small decline in groceries offset by a small increase in restaurant prices.

Within core categories, the gains were concentrated in a handful of volatile items: airline fares jumped 2.7 percent, apparel rose 1.0 percent, and transportation services increased 0.6 percent.

Working in the other direction, medical care fell 0.2 percent after a 0.5 percent increase in February, personal care declined 0.5 percent, and used cars and trucks dropped 0.4 percent for the second straight month. On balance, the decliners roughly offset the gainers, which is why core inflation held steady.

The three-month trend makes the headline–core divergence even starker. Over January through March, headline CPI averaged 0.47 percent per month - equivalent to a 5.6 percent annualized rate, well above the 3.3 percent year-over-year figure.

But virtually all of that acceleration comes from March’s energy spike. Core CPI over the same three months averaged just 0.23 percent per month, or about 2.8 percent annualized - barely above its 2.6 percent year-over-year pace. The underlying trend, in short, has not changed much.

Although the Federal Reserve officially targets the personal consumption expenditures price index (PCEPI) rather than the CPI, the distinction between headline and core is especially important this month.

An energy-driven price spike, however dramatic, is not the kind of broad-based inflation that would warrant a policy response. Markets seem to agree: the CME Group’s FedWatch tool now indicates that the Fed will almost certainly hold rates steady at its meeting later this month.

The latest labor market data reinforce the case for patience. March payrolls rebounded by 178,000 after February’s revised 133,000 decline, and the unemployment rate ticked down to 4.3 percent.

But perhaps more telling is what happened to wages: year-over-year earnings growth slowed to 3.5 percent, the weakest reading since May 2021. That suggests the nominal spending pressures that drive sustained inflation are easing, even as a geopolitical shock temporarily distorts the headline numbers.

The Fed can afford to look through the March energy spike. Core inflation is well-behaved, wage growth is moderating, and the 0.9 percent headline reading is a reflection of what is happening in the Strait of Hormuz, not in the domestic economy.

The real question is whether elevated energy costs linger long enough to raise inflation expectations. If they do, the calculus changes. But for now, the data suggest the Fed should hold steady.
